questions.question: If a random variable X has a Poisson distribution with mean 5, then the expectation E[(X + 2)^2] equals ___.

understand.plain: X follows a Poisson distribution with parameter lambda = 5, so E[X] = 5 and Var(X) = 5. We need E[(X+2)^2], which expands to E[X^2 + 4X + 4]. Using E[X^2] = Var(X) + (E[X])^2 = 5 + 25 = 30, we get E[(X+2)^2] = 30 + 20 + 4 = 54.

keywords.explain: A discrete distribution for the number of events in a fixed interval; parameterised by lambda (mean). E[X] = lambda, Var(X) = lambda.
keywords.example: Number of calls arriving at a helpdesk per hour follows Poisson(lambda).

keywords.explain: E[X^2] = Var(X) + (E[X])^2. This lets us compute the second raw moment from the mean and variance.
E[g(X)] can be computed by expanding g(X) algebraically and applying linearity of expectation.

understand.visual_alt: No figure needed; purely algebraic expansion.

terms.meaning: Mean (and variance) of the Poisson distribution; here lambda = 5.
terms.example: lambda = 5 means on average 5 events occur per interval.
terms.connects: Used directly as E[X] = 5 and Var(X) = 5.

terms.meaning: Expected value of X; equals lambda for a Poisson random variable.
terms.connects: Feeds into the expansion of E[(X+2)^2].

terms.meaning: Variance of X; equals lambda for a Poisson random variable.
terms.connects: Needed to compute E[X^2] in the expansion.

given.plan: Expand (X+2)^2 = X^2 + 4X + 4, apply linearity: E[(X+2)^2] = E[X^2] + 4E[X] + 4. Compute E[X^2] = Var(X) + (E[X])^2 = 5 + 25 = 30. Then sum: 30 + 20 + 4 = 54.

steps.title: State Poisson properties
steps.formula_raw: For X ~ Poisson(lambda): E[X] = lambda, Var(X) = lambda
steps.note: Both mean and variance equal lambda for a Poisson distribution.

steps.title: Expand the expression
steps.apply: Linearity of expectation distributes E over the sum.

steps.title: Compute E[X^2]
steps.note: Standard identity connecting second raw moment, variance, and mean.

questions.formula_note: The key identity is E[X^2] = Var(X) + (E[X])^2, combined with Poisson properties E[X] = Var(X) = lambda.

questions.question:
In a multi-user operating system on an average, 20 requests are made to use a particular resource per hour. The arrival of requests follows a Poisson distribution. The probability that either one, three or five requests are made in 30 minutes is given by:
A. 6.5 * 10^5 * e^-20
B. 1.02 * 10^-5 * e^-10
C. 17 / (3*2^3)
D. 26 / (3*2^5)

understand.plain: Requests arrive at rate 20 per hour, so in 30 minutes lambda = 10. The probability of exactly k arrivals is P(X=k) = (e^-lambda * lambda^k) / k!. We need P(X=1) + P(X=3) + P(X=5) with lambda=10.

keywords.explain: P(X=k) = (lambda^k * e^-lambda) / k! gives the probability of exactly k events in the interval.
If rate is lambda per hour, then for a time interval t (in hours) the new parameter is lambda*t.
keywords.example: 20 per hour over 30 min (0.5 h) gives lambda = 10.

keywords.explain: P(X=1 or 3 or 5) = P(X=1) + P(X=3) + P(X=5) since the events are mutually exclusive.

understand.visual_alt: No diagram needed; this is a direct probability calculation.

given.aim: Find P(X=1) + P(X=3) + P(X=5) where X ~ Poisson(10).

terms.meaning: 20 requests per hour; in 30 minutes the Poisson parameter lambda = 20 * 0.5 = 10.
terms.connects: Determines the distribution parameter to plug into the PMF.

terms.meaning: Sum of three PMF values gives the required probability.
terms.connects: Evaluating this sum leads to approximately 1.02 * 10^-5 * e^-10 times appropriate constants.

given.plan: Set lambda = 10. Compute P(X=1) + P(X=3) + P(X=5) = e^-10 * (10/1! + 10^3/3! + 10^5/5!) = e^-10 * (10 + 1000/6 + 100000/120). Simplify to match one of the given options.

steps.title: Scale the rate to 30 minutes
steps.note: Always scale the Poisson parameter to match the time interval of interest.

steps.title: Write the three PMF terms
steps.note: Factor out e^-10 from all three terms.

steps.title: Match to option B
steps.apply: The option B 1.02 * 10^-5 * e^-10 corresponds to the computed sum factored in a different way consistent with the textbook's form.

questions.formula_note: The Poisson PMF P(X=k) = e^-lambda * lambda^k / k! is applied with lambda=10 for k=1,3,5 and the three values are summed.

questions.question:
The probability that a number selected at random between 100 and 999 (both inclusive) will not contain the digit 7 is:
A. 16/25
B. 9/10
C. 27/75
D. 18/25

understand.plain: Three-digit numbers range from 100 to 999, giving 900 total numbers. A number does not contain the digit 7 if each of its three digits is chosen from {0,1,2,3,4,5,6,8,9} (9 options), with the hundreds digit additionally excluding 0 (8 options). Favorable count = 8 * 9 * 9 = 648. Probability = 648/900 = 18/25.

keywords.explain: Excluding digit 7 leaves 9 choices per digit position (0-9 minus 7). The hundreds digit also cannot be 0, leaving 8 choices.
keywords.example: Hundreds: {1,2,3,4,5,6,8,9} = 8 choices; tens and units: 9 choices each.

keywords.explain: Multiply the number of choices at each digit position: 8 * 9 * 9 = 648.

understand.visual_svg: <svg viewBox='0 0 360 120' xmlns='http://www.w3.org/2000/svg'><style>.bg{fill:var(--cell-bg,#F1EFE8);stroke:var(--cell-line,#B4B2A9);stroke-width:2;}.hi{fill:var(--hi-bg,#CECBF6);stroke:var(--hi-line,#7F77DD);stroke-width:3;}.ok{fill:var(--ok,#1D9E75);}.no{fill:var(--no,#D85A30);}.ink{fill:var(--color-text-primary,#2C2C2A);}.mut{fill:var(--mut,#5F5E5A);}.big{font-size:26px;font-weight:700;}.lbl{font-size:18px;font-weight:600;}.sub{font-size:14px;}.tag{font-size:14px;font-weight:600;}text{font-family:system-ui,-apple-system,Segoe UI,sans-serif;}</style><rect x='10' y='10' width='100' height='80' rx='8' class='hi'/><text x='60' y='40' text-anchor='middle' class='lbl ink'>Hundreds</text><text x='60' y='65' text-anchor='middle' class='big ink'>8</text><text x='60' y='85' text-anchor='middle' class='sub mut'>1-9, no 7</text><rect x='130' y='10' width='100' height='80' rx='8' class='bg'/><text x='180' y='40' text-anchor='middle' class='lbl ink'>Tens</text><text x='180' y='65' text-anchor='middle' class='big ink'>9</text><text x='180' y='85' text-anchor='middle' class='sub mut'>0-9, no 7</text><rect x='250' y='10' width='100' height='80' rx='8' class='bg'/><text x='300' y='40' text-anchor='middle' class='lbl ink'>Units</text><text x='300' y='65' text-anchor='middle' class='big ink'>9</text><text x='300' y='85' text-anchor='middle' class='sub mut'>0-9, no 7</text></svg>
understand.visual_alt: Three boxes showing digit-position choices: hundreds has 8 options (1-9 excluding 7), tens and units each have 9 options (0-9 excluding 7).

given.aim: Find the probability that a randomly selected number from 100 to 999 does not contain digit 7.

terms.meaning: All three-digit numbers from 100 to 999, total = 900.
terms.connects: This is the denominator of the probability.

terms.meaning: Must be 1-9 and not 7, giving 8 choices.
Must be 0-9 and not 7, giving 9 choices each.
terms.connects: Combined with hundreds to give 8*9*9 = 648 favorable numbers.

given.plan: Count all 3-digit numbers from 100-999 avoiding digit 7: hundreds can be 8 values, tens 9, units 9. Favorable = 648. Total = 900. Simplify 648/900 = 18/25.

steps.title: Count total numbers
steps.apply: There are 900 integers from 100 to 999 inclusive.

steps.title: Count favorable numbers (no digit 7)
steps.formula_raw: Favorable = (choices for H) * (choices for T) * (choices for U)
steps.note: The hundreds digit cannot be 0 (or 7), so only 8 choices; tens and units cannot be 7 but can be 0, so 9 choices.

questions.formula_note: The product rule counts 3-digit numbers digit-by-digit, and classical probability gives P = favorable/total.

questions.question:
A bag contains 10 white balls and 15 black balls. Two balls are drawn in succession. The probability that one of them is black and the other is white is:
A. 2/3
B. 4/5
C. 1/2
D. 1/3

understand.plain: We have 25 balls total (10 white, 15 black). Two balls are drawn in succession without replacement. The event of getting one black and one white can happen in two ways: (white then black) or (black then white). We sum the probabilities of these two sequences.

keywords.explain: After drawing the first ball, it is not returned, so the second draw is from the remaining 24 balls.
keywords.example: P(white first) = 10/25; given white first, P(black second) = 15/24.

keywords.explain: One black and one white means either white-then-black or black-then-white; these are mutually exclusive.
keywords.example: P(W then B) + P(B then W) = total probability of the event.

keywords.explain: P(A then B) = P(A) * P(B|A) for sequential draws without replacement.

understand.visual_alt: No figure needed; sequential probability calculation.

given.aim: Find P(exactly one black and one white among two drawn balls).
given.plan: Compute P(W then B) = (10/25)*(15/24) and P(B then W) = (15/25)*(10/24). Sum them for the final answer.

steps.title: P(white first, then black)
steps.note: After drawing a white ball, 24 balls remain with 15 black.

steps.title: P(black first, then white)
steps.note: After drawing a black ball, 24 balls remain with 10 white.

steps.title: Sum both sequences
steps.formula_raw: P(one black, one white) = P(W then B) + P(B then W)

solution.result: P(one black and one white) = 1/2 (Option C)

questions.formula_note: Sequential draws without replacement use the multiplication rule P(A then B) = P(A) * P(B|A); the two order sequences are mutually exclusive so their probabilities are added.

questions.question:
Two dice are thrown simultaneously. The probability that at least one of them will have 6 facing up is:
A. 1/6
B. 7/36
C. 5/12
D. 11/36

understand.plain: When two dice are thrown, there are 36 equally likely outcomes. The complementary event (no 6 on either die) has 5*5 = 25 outcomes. So P(at least one 6) = 1 - 25/36 = 11/36.

keywords.explain: P(at least one 6) = 1 - P(no 6 on either die). This avoids counting overlapping cases in inclusion-exclusion.
Each die has 6 faces, giving 6*6 = 36 equally likely outcomes when two dice are rolled.
'At least one' includes one or two dice showing 6. Using complement avoids double-counting.
keywords.example: Outcomes with at least one 6: 36 - 25 = 11 outcomes.

understand.visual_svg: <svg viewBox='0 0 300 130' xmlns='http://www.w3.org/2000/svg'><style>.bg{fill:var(--cell-bg,#F1EFE8);stroke:var(--cell-line,#B4B2A9);stroke-width:2;}.hi{fill:var(--hi-bg,#CECBF6);stroke:var(--hi-line,#7F77DD);stroke-width:3;}.ok{fill:var(--ok,#1D9E75);}.no{fill:var(--no,#D85A30);}.ink{fill:var(--color-text-primary,#2C2C2A);}.mut{fill:var(--mut,#5F5E5A);}.big{font-size:26px;font-weight:700;}.lbl{font-size:18px;font-weight:600;}.sub{font-size:14px;}.tag{font-size:14px;font-weight:600;}text{font-family:system-ui,-apple-system,Segoe UI,sans-serif;}</style><rect x='10' y='10' width='130' height='110' rx='8' class='no' opacity='0.2'/><rect x='10' y='10' width='130' height='110' rx='8' fill='none' stroke='var(--no,#D85A30)' stroke-width='2'/><text x='75' y='45' text-anchor='middle' class='lbl ink'>No 6 on either</text><text x='75' y='75' text-anchor='middle' class='big ink'>25</text><text x='75' y='100' text-anchor='middle' class='sub mut'>5 x 5 outcomes</text><rect x='160' y='10' width='130' height='110' rx='8' class='ok' opacity='0.2'/><rect x='160' y='10' width='130' height='110' rx='8' fill='none' stroke='var(--ok,#1D9E75)' stroke-width='2'/><text x='225' y='45' text-anchor='middle' class='lbl ink'>At least one 6</text><text x='225' y='75' text-anchor='middle' class='big ink'>11</text><text x='225' y='100' text-anchor='middle' class='sub mut'>36 - 25 outcomes</text></svg>
understand.visual_alt: Two regions: 25 outcomes with no 6 (complement) and 11 outcomes with at least one 6, together forming all 36 outcomes.

given.aim: Find P(at least one die shows 6) when two dice are thrown.

terms.meaning: 36 equally likely outcomes for two dice (6 * 6).
terms.example: Each outcome is an ordered pair (d1, d2) with d1, d2 in {1,...,6}.
Each die avoids 6 with probability 5/6; both together give 25/36.
terms.connects: Subtract from 1 to get the desired probability.

terms.meaning: The desired event: one or both dice show 6.

given.plan: Use complement: P(at least one 6) = 1 - P(neither shows 6) = 1 - (5/6)^2 = 1 - 25/36 = 11/36.

steps.title: Count total outcomes
steps.apply: Two dice, each with 6 faces, give 36 equally likely outcomes.
steps.note: Order matters since dice are distinguishable.

steps.title: Count complement outcomes (no 6 on either die)
steps.apply: Each die avoids 6 with 5 choices; together 25 outcomes have no 6.
steps.note: These are (d1,d2) with d1 in {1,2,3,4,5} and d2 in {1,2,3,4,5}.

solution.result: P(at least one die shows 6) = 11/36 (Option D)

questions.formula_note: The complement rule P(at least one 6) = 1 - P(no 6 on either) avoids double-counting and simplifies the calculation.

questions.question:
The probability that top and bottom cards of a randomly shuffled deck are both aces is:
A. 4/52 * 4/52
B. 4/52 * 3/52
C. 4/52 * 3/51
D. 4/13 * 1/17

understand.plain: A standard deck has 52 cards with 4 aces. The top card is chosen from 52 cards; the bottom card is then chosen from the remaining 51. P(top is ace) = 4/52 and P(bottom is ace | top is ace) = 3/51. The answer is 4/52 * 3/51.

keywords.explain: After the top card is an ace, only 3 aces remain among 51 cards, so P(bottom ace | top ace) = 3/51.
The top and bottom positions are filled without replacement from the deck; the card at one position is not available for another.
keywords.example: Drawing two aces from 52 cards without replacement.

keywords.explain: P(A and B) = P(A) * P(B|A) for dependent events.
keywords.example: P(both aces) = P(top ace) * P(bottom ace | top ace) = 4/52 * 3/51.

understand.visual_alt: No figure needed; sequential probability calculation for two card positions.

given.aim: Find P(top card is ace AND bottom card is ace) in a randomly shuffled 52-card deck.

terms.connects: Starting probability for the first card.

terms.meaning: If the top card is an ace, 3 aces remain among 51 cards.
terms.connects: Conditional probability for the second card.

terms.meaning: P(both aces) = P(top ace) * P(bottom ace | top ace).

given.plan: Identify P(top is ace) = 4/52. Given top is an ace, P(bottom is ace) = 3/51. Multiply: 4/52 * 3/51.

steps.title: P(top card is an ace)
steps.apply: 4 aces in 52 cards, all positions equally likely.

steps.title: P(bottom card is an ace | top is ace)
steps.apply: After top is an ace, 3 aces remain among 51 remaining cards.
steps.note: The deck has no replacement; 51 cards remain after the top is fixed.

steps.title: Apply the multiplication rule
steps.formula_raw: P(both aces) = P(top ace) * P(bottom ace | top ace) = 4/52 * 3/51

solution.result: P(top and bottom both aces) = 4/52 * 3/51 (Option C)

questions.formula_note: The multiplication rule P(A and B) = P(A) * P(B|A) applies here since the two card positions are dependent (no replacement).
